How does what we eat
affect our planet?

by Natálie Hluší

About this project

This project has origin in a topic of personal interest. After being overwhelmed by the noise of social media, I went vegan for several months out of concern for emissions, pollution, and sustainability. I ended up facing some health issues that might have or might not have been connected to my diet changes. That seemed like a big risk, considering that I changed my diet only because of a common "Instagram theme". That experience left me with two questions: Does going vegan actually matter at a global scale? And if yes, what are the biggest contributors to the pollution? I'm going to answer these questions in this project.

Figure 1: Food Emissions

Beef emits 20× more per kilogram than peas

This chart ranks all 43 foods by greenhouse gas emissions per kg. Animal products dominate; but the surprises are in the middle.

Dark chocolate (18.7 kg) and coffee (16.5 kg) both beat pork (7.2 kg). Not all plant foods are low-carbon! Average animal food: 16.4 kg CO₂eq/kg vs. plant average of only 2.8 kg.

Beef: 59.6 kg CO₂eq/kg Nuts: 0.2 kg CO₂eq/kg
Figure 2: Supply-Chain Stages

The farm causes 58% of emissions (not the packaging)

Each panel shows one food broken into 7 supply-chain stages. For beef, farming alone drives 39.4 of its 59.6 kg total. Land use change adds another 16.3 kg.

Transport accounts for just 3.3% of food emissions. Buying local matters far less than what you actually buy.

Farm: 58% · Transport: 3.3%
Figure 3: Global CO₂

Food is one part of a larger crisis

Food systems account for ~26% of all global greenhouse gas emissions. This map shows who drives the rest.

In 2000, the USA was the world's largest single emitter at 304 billion tons. Russia (83B) and Germany (76B) ranked 2nd and 3rd — China (75B) was 4th at that time

USA: 304B tons (2000) China: 75B tons (2000)
Figure 4: Development & Health

How do GDP, sanitation, and water access relate to infant mortality?

This chart explores four development indicators across countries in the dataset: GDP per capita, sanitation coverage, clean water access, and infant mortality. Average clean water access is 64.6%.

Drag on any axis to filter countries by a range of values and trace how the indicators co-vary.

What does the data actually say?

After examining all charts, what stands out...


• Replacing beef with tofu cuts GHG by ~95% per kg of protein
• Buying local has minimal effect. The farm stage causes 58% of food emissions. What you eat matters far more than where it came from.

Going vegan is not the only answer. But the data is clear; reducing beef and lamb has an outsized impact.

Data sources: Food Production Emissions CO₂ Emissions by Country Water Pollution & Disease
GitHub Repository
